data.title: S&P Global Services PMI: Growth Softened Slightly In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The September U.S. Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) from S&P Global came in at 54.2 ,down slightly from 54.5 in August. The reading marks the 32nd consecutive month of expansion and was higher than the forecast of 53.9.

From the latest press release, Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said:

“Service sector growth softened slightly in September but remained strong enough to round off an impressive performance over the third quarter a whole. Combined with sustained growth in the manufacturing sector, the expansion of service sector activity is indicative of robust third quarter annualized GDP growth of around 2.5%.

“Growth is being fueled principally by rising financial services and tech sector activity, though we are also seeing more signs of improving demand for consumer-facing services such as leisure and recreation, likely linked in part to lower interest rates. Lower borrowing costs have also fed through to a broad based improvement in business optimism about the outlook for the next 12 months.

“Disappointingly, the improvement in business optimism failed to spur more jobs growth, with hiring almost stalling in a sign of further labor market malaise as companies often focused on running more efficiently amid uncertain trading conditions.

“A further ongoing source of concern from the surveys are heightened cost pressures which survey respondents have attributed to tariffs. Input costs rose sharply again in September as import levies were seen to have again fed through from goods to services. However, rates charged for services rose at the slowest rate for five months in a welcome sign that some of these tariff price pressures in supply chains are starting to moderate.

Background on the S&P Global US Services PMI

The S&P Global US Services PMI™ measures the activity level of purchasing managers in the services sector through a questionnaire of ~400 service sector companies. The sectors covered include consumer (excluding retail), transportation, information, communication, finance, insurance, real estate, and business services.

The S&P Services PMI is a diffusion index, meaning that a reading above 50 indicates expansion in the sector compared to the previous month and a reading below 50 indicates contraction.

Here is a snapshot of the series since mid-2012.

Here is an overlay with the equivalent PMI survey conducted by the Institute for Supply Management, which it refers to as "non-manufacturing" (see our full article on this series here). Over its history, the ISM metric has been significantly the more volatile of the two.

The next chart uses a three-month moving average of the two rather volatile series to facilitate our understanding of the current trend.

S&P Global PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by S&P Global. See the latest update on the Manufacturing PMI here.

data.title: TQQQ: An Alpha Opportunity
data.description:
There are two strong arguments for owning a tech-focused leveraged ETF product such as the ProShares UltraPro QQQ (NASDAQ:TQQQ): 1) AI spending is set to accelerate in the years ahead, which will benefit companies with considerable exposure to this specific sector of the U.S. economy, especially Magnificent 7 firms, and 2) the recent flurry of deals affecting the neocloud businesses, including companies that run GPU-as-a-Service business models such as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius Group (NBIS), highlights that the upcoming third-quarter earnings season could create alpha for TQQQ investors.

ChatGPT creator OpenAI is on a massive spending spree in order to prepare the business for the age of super intelligence, which highlights sustained growth momentum for tech companies as well. The ProShares UltraPro QQQ could be a potentially lucrative investment product for investors that want to use the leveraged ETF as a tactical asset allocation instrument in preparation for a strong third-quarter earnings season.

Previous Rating

In my last review of the ProShares UltraPro QQQ -- Material Outperformance Potential -- I highlighted the potential of artificial intelligence as a major growth catalyst for the ETF's net asset value growth: since then, shares have increased 27.4% in value, and more upside may lurk just around the corner as companies get ready to open their books for Q3. Recently, we have seen multi-billion-dollar deals in the Data Center industry and deal sizes are starting to get really big. This creates a favorable backdrop for investors that want to invest, in anticipation of a strong earnings season, in the tech sector.

Increasing Concentration Of Magnificent 7 Stocks, Strong Tech Earnings Season Ahead

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ seeks to 3x the daily performance of the Nasdaq-100 Index and is therefore a leveraged ETF product with above-average levels of volatility. However, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ could be a potentially suitable investment product for investors that expect top results from the country's leading suite of technology companies.

The main reason why I like the ProShares UltraPro QQQ is because the ETF is a directional bet on the technology industry, and especially on companies that have considerable AI exposure, such as the Magnificent 7. This 'mag-7' group of stocks includes Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META).

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has been heavily concentrated in Magnificent 7 stocks... all of which have significant exposure to the theme of artificial intelligence. Since my last coverage on the ProShares UltraPro QQQ, however, the ETF has seen a material increase in the concentration of Magnificent 7 stocks, due to the broad rise in market valuations.

A number of artificial intelligence stocks have seen record highs lately, including Nvidia, which is the world’s largest AI GPU maker, and Alphabet, which recently received a favorable Chrome ruling from a U.S. federal court.

Nvidia currently has a portfolio rating of 9.2%, compared to a 5.8% portfolio share in my last coverage in June. Microsoft and Apple, the ETF’s second- and third-largest holdings, increased their allocations by 3.2 PP and 2.6 PP respectively. TQQQ's top ten holdings now represent 50.8% of all the ETF’s investments, compared to just 32.2% previously. The Magnificent 7's share has gone up to 39.8%, showing an increase of 14.6 PP since my last coverage.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has therefore been a major beneficiary of soaring AI spending in the Data Center business, but recent deals indicate that we are still very much at the beginning of a new age of superintelligence... creating upside revaluation potential as the total addressable market expands.

The AI data center industry has seen major deals recently, which included a $17.4B (+$2.0B deal upside) agreement between Nebius and Microsoft for the provision of cloud compute -- Microsoft Deal Changes Everything -- as well as a $6.3B deal between Nvidia and CoreWeave. Additionally, Nvidia and OpenAI agreed on a $100B investment deal -- which is part of OpenAI's grand plan to build out its Data Center footprint to the tune of 850B over the next several years.

According to business consulting company McKinsey, global Data Center capacity demand is set for explosive growth until the end of the decade, and this demand growth is chiefly driven by AI workloads. These workloads are expected to see a factor increase of 3.5X between FY 2025 and FY 2030, and create the backdrop for sustained growth in the Magnificent 7 investment category.

With deal sizes in the Data Center industry increasing rapidly, strong results for Magnificent 7 companies can be expected for the upcoming earnings season... which starts later this month. Investors that want to play the potential upside that comes with better-than-expected earnings reports for Q3 '25, may want to consider buying into the ProShares UltraPro QQQ.

TQQQ: Strong Performance History

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ currently has a net asset value of $106.09/share, and the ETF is trading at a small NAV premium of 0.04%. For comparison, the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) is trading at a 0.01% premium and offers investors essentially the same Nasdaq-100 exposure, just without the added kicker of leverage that the TQQQ is using. I covered the QQQ here and continue to recommend the ETF as a strong buy option, even at all-time highs, for investors that are a little more risk-averse: A Growth ETF With An AI Catalyst.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has significantly outperformed the Invesco QQQ Trust, which is due to the leveraged nature of the TQQQ and the fact that the tech sector has delivered very strong returns, especially since the advent of ChatGPT in 2022. The ProShares UltraPro QQQ, in the context of recent AI deals, has continual outperformance potential, in my opinion.

Risks with TQQQ

A material risk factor for investors investing in a leveraged 3x investment product is volatility drag which is due to the daily rebalancing of an ETF’s leverage position. Volatility drag means investors may lose out on returns in an exceptionally volatile market -- as the TQQQ seeks to 3x the daily performance of the Nasdaq- 100 -- which may lead to underperformance long term. A leveraged ETF magnifies the volatility of the underlying index and is therefore mainly suitable for investors seeking a short-term asset allocation tool. The risks associated with leveraged ETFs need to be clearly understood and include the potential for significant capital loss.

Final thoughts

The age of superintelligence is upon us, and the ProShares UltraPro QQQ could be a potentially interesting investment product for investors that have a very high risk tolerance and are willing to accept a high degree of volatility in their portfolios.

Given the progressively increasing deal sizes for companies that run GPU-as-a-Service business models, and OpenAI's massive $850B spending spree in order to build out its Data Center footprint, there is a strong likelihood, in my opinion, that the third-quarter earnings season is going to be very strong for tech companies.

Nvidia is trading near all-time highs and has recently struck major deals with neocloud businesses like CoreWeave as well as OpenAI. AI-spending is set to accelerate further, in my opinion, potentially leading to more deals benefiting the Magnificent 7 companies. I believe TQQQ could be a major winner in the upcoming earnings season, and I confirm my strong buy rating.

data.title: European Bank Stocks Extend Rally In Q3, Surpassing U.S., Asia Rivals
data.description:
European banks extended their stock rally in the third quarter, surging ahead of US and Asian counterparts.

The S&P Europe BMI Banks index outperformed US and APAC bank indexes, rising 14.71% in the three-month period. The index has risen 73.89% year to date, versus a 25.48% increase for the US S&P 500 Bank index and a 22.25% rise for the S&P Pan Asia BMI Banks index, S&P Global Market Intelligence data shows.

European banking stocks have been propelled by buoyant quarterly profits, supported by strong noninterest income that helped offset the impact of declining interest rates on lending income. Banks also benefited from a surge in trading income amid heightened market volatility.

Improved profitability and robust capital buffers position mean European banks are well placed to absorb potentially higher credit losses due to rising trade tensions, according to the European Banking Authority's latest EU-wide bank stress test. This could lead to higher dividends and buybacks by the region's banks.

Bank M&A activity in Europe also continues to gather steam. Recent transactions include Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA (OTCPK:BMDPF) gaining control of Italian peer Mediobanca Banca di Credito Finanziario SpA (OTCPK:MDIBF)(OTCPK:MDIBY) and Spanish group Banco Santander SA's (SAN) £2.65 billion deal to acquire UK-based TSB Banking Group PLC from Banco de Sabadell SA.

Spain's four largest banks were also among the best-performing European banking stocks during the quarter.

Santander logged a total return of 26.3%, while Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA) and Sabadell registered gains of 25.2% and 24.7%, respectively. CaixaBank SA (OTCPK:CIXPF)(OTCPK:CAIXY) had a total return of 21.6%, Market Intelligence data shows.

Strong economic growth in Spain is expected to fuel growth and further bolster banks' profitability rebound as they navigate challenging geopolitical and global trade tensions.

Two Italian banks also made the top 15 list. Banco BPM SpA (OTCPK:BNCZF), which avoided a takeover by larger peer UniCredit SpA (OTCPK:UNCFF)(OTCPK:UNCRY), had a total return of 28.4%. BPER Banca SpA (OTCPK:BPXXY), which bought Banca Popolare di Sondrio SpA, recorded a 22.3% gain.

Greece-based CrediaBank SA was the best-performing European bank stock in the third quarter, with a total return of 99.8%. The company, formerly known as Attica Bank, recently signed a put option agreement to acquire a 70.03% stake in HSBC Holdings PLC's (HSBC) Malta unit for €200 million. Larger domestic peer Piraeus Financial Holdings SA (OTCPK:BPIRF)(OTCPK:BPIRY) delivered gains of 22.6% as it reported a higher-than-expected net interest income (NII) result for the second quarter.

Sweden's TF Bank AB (publ) ranked second on the list. The digital bank, which began trading on the Nasdaq Stockholm in July following a 3:1 stock split, plans to change its corporate name to Avarda Bank AB.

QNB Bank AS, the Turkish unit of Qatar National Bank QPSC, rounded out the top three, with a total return of 51.7%.

Poland-based Bank Polska Kasa Opieki SA (OTCPK:BKPKF) and Bank Handlowy w Warszawie SAregistered negative shareholder returns of 5.6% and 4.7%, respectively. Shares in the country's banks are yet to rebound after plummeting Aug. 22, following government proposals to increase the corporate income tax rate for banks to help fund defense and healthcare.

Some savings banks in Norway also took a hit during the quarter, with SpareBank 1 Nord-Norge (OTCPK:SPXXF), SpareBank 1 Sør-Norge ASA (OTCPK:SBRKF) and SpareBank 1 Østlandet (OTCPK:SPRBF) registering negative total returns ranging from 3.3% to 4.4%. DNB Bank ASA (OTCPK:DNBBF)(OTCPK:DNBBY), Norway's largest bank, logged a negative return of 2.5%. The Norges Bank in September delivered a quarter-percentage-point cut in interest rates to 4%, citing an uncertain economic outlook, but projected only one rate cut per year in the coming three years.

Also on the list are Turkey-based Akbank TAS (OTCQX:AKBTY)and Türkiye Vakiflar Bankasi TAO (OTC:TKYVY), with respective negative returns of 8.1% and 3.3%, respectively. The Turkish BIST Banks Index tumbled in September after a court ordered the removal of the Istanbul head of the main opposition Republican People's Party. The ruling dealt a fresh judicial setback to opponents of President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan, news outlets including Reuters and Bloomberg News wrote.

data.title: Neo-Clouds With Gold AI Linings
data.description:
By Yan Taw (YT) Boon

A new breed of smaller cloud service providers is offering high-performance AI computing at a fraction of what Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) charge.

Last month, U.S. President Donald Trump brought together 33 of Silicon Valley’s most influential leaders to address a burgeoning challenge: How to meet insatiable demand for data-processing power in the age of artificial intelligence?

It’s a question worthy of such a summit. Even cloud service giants like Amazon, Google and Microsoft (MSFT) are hitting data-processing bottlenecks. While those so-called hyperscalers continue to invest heavily in custom chips, power systems and next-generation data center architecture, no single player can meet the scale and complexity of AI’s demands alone. Instead, tech’s titans are now looking to forge strategic partnerships with a new breed of “neo-cloud” providers.

Unlike the hyperscalers, which offer a broad range of cloud solutions, neo-cloud providers simply rent raw computing power for AI and advanced applications - a business model known as “GPUs-as-a-service.”

Instead of relying on massive, centralized data centers, these scrappy operators deploy their data-crunching power across thousands of smaller centers or regional hubs, increasing the flexibility and resilience of their networks - not unlike high-frequency traders who aim to shave off precious milliseconds by placing their servers closer to securities exchanges.

By partnering with neo-cloud newcomers, such as Nebius (NBIS) and CoreWeave (CRWV), behemoths like Microsoft aim to handle daunting AI workloads at increasing scale. For example, Microsoft recently signed a deal with Nebius - worth up to $20 billion over five years - under which Microsoft will lease computing power from Nebius’ new data center in Vineland, New Jersey.1

Meanwhile, leading AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) has taken a significant equity stake in CoreWeave, worth roughly $3 billion at the end of June, and has also agreed to purchase up to $6.3 billion in unsold data-processing capacity from the company through the spring of 2032.2 (Nvidia is a shareholder in Nebius as well.)

For smaller enterprises, we believe neo-clouds have the potential to lower the barriers to entry for high-performance AI and advanced analytics. Thanks to their streamlined operations, neo-cloud providers can rent their GPUs at an hourly rate between two and seven times lower than what hyperscalers typically charge,3 potentially allowing young businesses to get on their feet without having to make large upfront investments or long-term commitments in cutting-edge technology.

Furthermore, neo-clouds can offer industry-specific data-processing power, potentially giving them an additional edge over the hyperscalers. For example, Nebius offers cloud services expressly designed to support the e-commerce industry - a big reason it recently landed Shopify (SHOP) as a client.4

Yet the benefits of neo-clouds extend well beyond technical agility, in our view. Amid broader trends toward de-globalization and regional data sovereignty, we believe newer cloud service players may be better positioned than the hyperscalers - and perhaps more trusted - to comply with local data governance and environmental mandates, including Europe’s General Data Protection Regulation, Artificial Intelligence Act, and Digital Operation Resilience Act. CoreWeave, for one, aims to build advanced AI “factories” in the UK powered by renewable energy and advanced cooling technology to limit water consumption.5

Over the next five years, the total addressable GPU-as-a-service market is expected to grow at 35% a year, reaching $260 billion by 2030.6 We will be keeping an eye on those providers with strong relationships with chipmakers and hyperscalers, as well as those with differentiated software stacks catering to specific industries. For thematic tech investors, we believe the forecast calls for a strong chance of neo-clouds.

1“Microsoft taps Nebius to supply up to $20bn of AI computing power,” Financial Times, September 9, 2025;

2“CoreWeave’s stock rallies on disclosure of $6.3 billion order from Nvidia,” CNBC, September 15, 2025;

3“What is a Neocloud? The Rise of GPU-only Clouds,” Thunder Compute, April 25, 2025;

4“Augmented Commerce: Machine Learning at Shopify,” July 4, 2025;

5CoreWeave Announces £1.5 Billion Commitment to Power UK AI Innovation and Growth Through Sustainable Computing, September 16, 2025;

6Nebius Group N.V., December 31, 2024.
